narrator_selection_prompt: You’ve heard them all. Who do you want by your side?

ship_computer_lines.text: Systems online. Hull integrity: marginal. Credits: minimal. One station in sensor range.
Three officers responded to your posting. They are en route. For now, it’s just us.
Controls are live. WASD to fly, left-click to set course. The station ahead is your only option. Dock with E when close.
NOTICE: Instrument calibration variance detected. Non-critical. Logging.
Cruise drive available. Hold C to engage sustained thrust.
Tutorial complete. {credits_earned} credits earned. {nodes_visited} systems explored. {combats_won} hostiles eliminated. {modules_equipped} modules installed.

selection_intros.quote: I deal in probabilities, Captain. Every route has a number. Every risk has a margin. Let me run yours.
Twenty years in the Concord fleet. I know these lanes, these stations, these factions. Need someone steady.
I’ve been everywhere they said not to go. The maps are wrong in the best ways. I notice things most people walk past.

fo_hail_lines.text: Captain, I’ve reviewed your route history. Whoever was running your numbers before got you into this. I can get you out.
I’ve seen contractors lose their shirts on the frontier. The lanes here are rough but honest. You need someone who knows when to hold course.
Your posting said ‘frontier, open-ended.’ That’s the only kind I answer. Everyone else is afraid of the edges.

wrong_station_lines.text: The numbers don’t work here. We’d lose credits selling at this station. I’d suggest heading to {station} instead.
Wrong port for this cargo, Captain. We need to keep moving — {station} has better demand.
Not the right place for what we’re carrying. Let’s try {station} — I’ve got a feeling.

tutorial_lines.text: Captain, this is Maren Voss. I pulled your financials before I hailed — your burn rate gives you maybe forty hours before insolvency.
Captain, this is Maren Voss. I’ve seen crews in worse shape, but not many. Your situation is precarious and the frontier doesn’t forgive hesitation.
Captain, this is Maren Voss. I picked up your distress beacon three systems out — something about the signal drew me in. You’re in rough shape, but you’re alive.
Captain, Maren Voss here. Your ledger reads like a cautionary tale — revenue zero, overhead climbing. We have a narrow window before the math turns fatal.
Captain, Maren Voss. I’ve pulled people out of tighter spots than this, but only the ones who listened. The frontier is unforgiving — let’s move.
Captain, this is Maren Voss. Your beacon was faint, but I follow faint signals — they lead to the most interesting places. You need help, and I’m curious.
Maren Voss, responding to your distress frequency. I ran a survival projection on your current resources — the confidence interval is not encouraging without intervention.
Maren Voss, Captain. I know desperation when I see it — served with enough crews on their last legs. But last legs can still walk if someone steadies them.
Maren Voss here, Captain. Your signal had a quality to it — not just distress, something else. Determination, maybe. Either way, I’m here.
Low credits, empty hold, one station in range. But the local price spreads show exploitable inefficiency. Dock up and I’ll walk you through the data.
Low credits, empty hold, one station in range. I’ve started from less. That station’s your lifeline — dock up and we’ll secure a foothold.
Low credits, empty hold, one station in range. But every trader I’ve admired started exactly like this. Dock up — let’s see what they’ve got.
One station, minimal capital, zero inventory. The constraints are tight but solvable. Dock there and I’ll identify the optimal first move.
Not much to work with — one station, thin credits, empty cargo bay. But I’ve built careers from less. Dock up and let’s get started.
Almost nothing in the hold, almost nothing in the bank. But that station is right there, and every great journey starts with the first port. Let’s dock.
The warfront has disrupted supply chains — mining shipments are backed up 40%. But supply disruption creates price asymmetry, and price asymmetry creates margin.
Warfront’s got the mining convoys rerouting. Prices are unstable, but I’ve traded through three conflicts — instability is where steady hands make their credits.
Something’s off with the prices here — the warfront has everything shuffled around. Chaos like this is where the real finds hide.
High stock means surplus — buy low here. Cross-reference with demand at the next port and the expected margin is 73%. Statistically sound.
When a station’s overflowing with goods, prices drop. Buy here, haul it somewhere that’s running short. Oldest trick on the frontier and it still works.
See all that surplus? Means it’s cheap. Somewhere out there, a station is desperate for exactly this. That gap between plenty and scarcity — that’s our opening.
Surplus drives prices down — basic supply economics. The station is practically giving this away. Buy here, sell where inventory is thin, and pocket the delta.
They’ve got more than they can use here. That means low prices for us. Haul it to a station that’s running low and they’ll pay a premium.
Look at those stockpiles — this station is drowning in goods. Cheap for us, valuable somewhere else. The trick is finding who needs what.
Price inversions are the foundation of every profitable trade route. This station’s oversupply is our buy signal. Acquire low, transport, sell at equilibrium.
Simple math, Captain — too much supply means cheap cargo. Another port will be short on exactly this. Buy, fly, sell. That’s the game.
Every station tells a story with its prices. This one says ‘too much.’ Somewhere out there, another station is saying ‘not enough.’ We’re the bridge.
Good call. Price-to-weight ratio is optimal. My model gives 73% confidence the margin holds at the next port.
Solid buy, Captain. I’ve run this kind of cargo before — it moves well on the frontier. Let’s not sit on it.
Nice pick. I’ve got a good feeling about this haul — something tells me the next port will want it badly.
Acquisition logged. Unit cost is well below the regional median — the probability of a profitable resale is high.
Good instinct, Captain. That cargo has legs — it’ll sell fast at the right port. Time to move.
That felt right. The hold’s loaded, the price was fair, and somewhere a station needs exactly this. Let’s go find them.
Anomalous reading — scanner dropout lasting 0.3 seconds during transit. Statistically insignificant, but I’m logging it for pattern analysis.
Scanner flickered during that jump. Probably lane interference — I’ve seen it before on older routes. Keeping an eye on it.
Did you feel that? The scanner went quiet for a heartbeat during transit. Like something was listening back. Probably nothing, but… interesting.
New station, new price sheet. Compare their buy price against our acquisition cost — any positive delta is pure profit.
We’re docked. Check their prices — if they’re paying more than we spent, offload the cargo. No reason to hold when the margin’s there.
New station, new opportunities. Check the sell prices — if they want what we’re carrying, this could be our first real score.
Docking complete. Pull up the market tab and compare their offer against what we paid. If the spread is positive, execute the sale.
We’re in. Time to check if this port is buying what we’re selling. If the price is right, don’t hesitate — offload and lock in the profit.
Let’s see what this station thinks our cargo is worth. If the numbers look good, this is where we turn a haul into real credits.
Market data loading. Cross-reference their buy price with our cost basis — any margin above zero validates the route hypothesis.
New port, Captain. Check the boards — see what they’re paying. If it’s more than we spent, that’s money in the bank.
Every station has different appetites. Let’s find out if this one is hungry for what we brought. Check those sell prices.
Profit logged. Actual margin deviated only 2 credits from my projection. The predictive model is validated.
Credits in the account. That’s how it starts — one clean run. Stack enough of these and nobody can touch you.
We made money! First trade, first profit. The galaxy just got a little bigger for us.
Transaction complete — net positive. The margin confirms the route’s viability. First data point in what should be a profitable trend.
There it is — first profit on the books. Every empire starts with one good trade. You’ve got the instinct, Captain.
Credits! Real credits, earned the honest way. That feeling? That’s the start of something. The frontier just opened up a little wider.
Revenue exceeds cost — trade loop validated. Repeating this pattern at scale is how small operators become large ones.
Profit’s profit, Captain. Doesn’t matter if it’s small — what matters is you made it. Now do it again, and again after that.
Look at that — we actually turned a profit. First of many, I hope. Every credit is fuel for the next discovery.
Active warfront detected between local factions — that’s the variable distorting the price matrix. Each faction controls territory and applies tariff modifiers.
Those factions are at war — that’s why the prices are all over the place. Each one holds territory and charges tariffs. Pick your friends carefully.
The factions here are fighting over these lanes, which is why everything feels unsettled. Each one claims territory and sets their own tariffs.
Reputation is the key variable — it determines tariff rates and unlocks faction-specific technology. Optimize your trade partners to maximize access.
Your standing with each faction sets your tariff rate and what tech they’ll sell you. Loyalty matters out here — earn it through trade.
The more a faction trusts you, the better your tariff rates and the more of their tech they’ll share. Every trade is a conversation.
Our data set is too small for reliable projections. More systems surveyed means tighter confidence intervals on trade routes.
We need to scout more systems before we commit to a strategy. Knowing the terrain is half the battle out here.
There’s so much we haven’t seen yet. Let’s push further out before we settle into a routine — the best discoveries come early.
Insufficient sample size for route optimization. Expanding our surveyed systems will reduce projection error significantly.
Can’t plan a campaign from one outpost. We need eyes on more systems — the more ground we cover, the better our options.
We’ve barely scratched the surface. The real opportunities are out past what we know — let’s keep moving and see what we find.
Current data coverage is below the threshold for reliable modeling. Each new system we survey improves every projection we make.
A good captain knows the territory. We’ve only seen a fraction of what’s out here — time to broaden our horizons.
Every system we haven’t visited is a question waiting to be answered. I say we go ask a few more.
Full galactic overlay loaded. Faction boundaries, trade lanes, contested zones — every data point we need to model optimal routes.
There’s the big picture. Faction borders, trade lanes, hot zones. I’ve fought in sectors like these — knowing the map keeps you alive.
Look at that — the whole galaxy laid out. Faction colors, trade lanes, the dark spaces in between. Every blank spot is somewhere we haven’t been yet.
Hostile signature on sensors. Intercept trajectory confirmed — probability of engagement: 94%.
Contact bearing hostile. They’re moving to intercept — I know that approach pattern. Get ready, Captain.
Something just lit up on the scanner — hostile, and heading straight for us. This doesn’t feel friendly.
Threat vector detected — single hostile on intercept course. Engagement probability exceeds 90%. Recommend combat readiness.
Hostile inbound, Captain. Standard aggressor profile — they’ve already committed to the attack run. Brace for contact.
That blip on the scanner isn’t friendly — it just changed course toward us. Whatever it wants, I don’t think it’s trade.
Sensor alert — hostile contact, closing fast. Trajectory analysis leaves no ambiguity. This is an engagement.
We’ve got company, Captain — hostile, on approach. I’ve seen that flight pattern a hundred times. They’re coming for us.
Scanner just went hot — something aggressive is heading our way. My instincts say fight, not flight. Your call, Captain.
Captain, I’m tracking that contact. Standard pirate — weak shields, no armor plating. You can take them. Close to weapons range and engage.
Threat eliminated. Combat data logged. But your hull took hits — structural integrity is compromised.
Clean kill, Captain. But look at that hull damage. Next time might not be so easy.
It’s gone. But so is some of our hull. That was closer than I’d like.
Dock at any station to repair. Also noted the fuel gauge — worth topping off when we’re there.
We need repairs, Captain. Find a station and dock. And top off the fuel while we’re at it.
We should find a station and patch up. Keep an eye on fuel too — combat burns through reserves.
That fight exposed a weakness. Your ship has module slots — empty ones. The salvaged module from that wreck would fit.
That pirate nearly had us because we’re running bare. Module slots are empty. The salvage from the wreckage could change that.
We survived, but barely. Your ship has empty module slots. I found something in the wreckage that would fit.
Better. But the good modules? Factions guard those. You’ll need to earn their trust — or find another way.
That’ll help. But the real firepower is faction-locked. Earn reputation or research alternatives.
Feels different already. But this is basic kit. The interesting modules are out there — behind faction walls and research trees.
Captain… your drive’s harmonic signature doesn’t match any registry I’ve cross-referenced. The resonance pattern is… unusual. Like it’s listening.
Three completed runs on this route gives us a statistically valid sample. Manual operation is no longer the efficient choice.
You’ve proven this route works. Running it by hand every time is a waste of your talents, Captain.
We’ve run this route three times now — it’s a known quantity. Why keep doing it ourselves when there’s so much else to explore?
Programs automate trade routes. One program earns while you focus on something more important.
Trade programs run routes automatically. Set one up and go find what the galaxy is hiding.
A TradeCharter handles the route. You handle the adventure.
One route running. Revenue accumulating passively. Now imagine ten. That’s an empire.
Route’s running. Credits rolling in. One route is good. Ten routes is an empire.
There it goes, working for us. One route running itself. Imagine what we could build.
Factions post commissions — bounties, deliveries, escort jobs. They pay well and build your reputation.
There’s work posted on the boards — commissions from the factions. Bounties, hauling jobs, escorts.
Factions need freelancers. Commissions are posted — deliveries, bounties, all kinds of work.
There are places the lanes don’t reach. Your drive can go further. Some signals only exist in deep space.
The lane network ends, but the galaxy doesn’t. Your drive can push beyond — into uncharted territory.
The mapped lanes are just the surface. Your drive hums in a way that suggests it knows a deeper path.
I’ve been analyzing your drive’s resonance patterns. They don’t match anything in the registry. This ship carries something that wasn’t built in this century.
Something’s been nagging me. Your drive signature — it’s not standard issue. I’ve served on a hundred ships and never seen readings like these.
I found a match for that harmonic anomaly. One match. In a fragment from a pre-Concord survey station. 340 years old.
Whatever built this drive understood something about space that we don’t. The answers are out there.
I don’t think we’re the first to travel these lanes. Someone was here before. Long before.
Something built this. Something that understood space in ways we’re only beginning to grasp. The edges of the map aren’t the edge of the story.
The galaxy is yours, Captain. I’ll keep analyzing.
You’ve got the basics, Captain. From here, it’s your call.
No more hand-holding. The stars are yours. I’ll be here when they surprise you.
Captain’s Commission earned. You’ve proven yourself on the frontier.
Captain’s Commission. You’ve earned the trust of your crew.
Captain’s Commission. The frontier is wide open. Let’s see what’s out there.
